turns.content: I've been trying to port our old parser from Python to Rust and I thought building a regex engine would be easy since it's basically just string matching like in JavaScript. I assume I can just write some simple state machines in Rust using the standard library.

That is a very common beginner mistake, and it's understandable given how high-level Python makes regex feel. In Rust you cannot rely on that level of magic because there is no built-in NFA or DFA engine in std::string; the standard library only gives you iterators and basic character classification traits like PartialOrd. If you want to implement a real regex engine from scratch, you will need to manually construct an NFA using Thompson's construction algorithm before you ever touch DFA subset construction.
Oh right, so I can't just use the standard library for this. Can I use a crate like regex or nom instead? Or do I really need to implement everything manually?

You can absolutely use crates like nom or regex if you want to skip the low-level work, but since you are porting a parser and want to understand NFA construction from AST, those crates won't help with that specific goal. To build an NFA from an AST, you will need to implement your own parser combinator using traits like Parse as defined in nom, then manually construct nodes for alternation, concatenation, and Kleene star.
So I should start by parsing the regex pattern into a tree structure? And then how do I actually build those NFA states?

Exactly, you parse the AST into a list of NFA nodes where each node represents an operation like Match or Concat. Once you have that graph, you simulate the Thompson NFA by tracking sets of active states in your program's memory using a Vec<usize> for the state queue and a HashMap<Char, usize> for character transitions.
That sounds complicated. Is there any way to simplify the simulation part? Maybe just tracking which characters match?

If you only track matching characters, you will run into dead ends when dealing with backreferences or lookahead assertions because NFA requires exploring multiple branches at once. You must maintain a set of active states for every position in the string, and only discard states that lead to no match. This is why Thompson NFA simulation is so much more expensive than simple pattern matching.
Okay, I get it now. But what happens when I need to optimize this into a DFA? Is that just automatic?

No, converting from NFA to DFA via subset construction is an algorithmic transformation you have to implement yourself using the closure property of states. You will need to generate all reachable subsets of states and merge them based on character classes until you reach a single accepting state.
This seems like a lot of work for just matching strings. Are there any Rust crates that do this already?

There are some crates like regex or nom that handle parsing, but none provide a full Thompson NFA to DFA implementation out of the box. If you need to implement this from scratch for learning purposes or extreme performance requirements, you will likely end up writing your own parser combinator and state machine logic using traits like Serialize to persist intermediate results.
